title: Git Essentials for Daily Work
subtitle: The commands you'll actually use on a working team
description: A hands-on short course covering the 90% of Git that shows up in daily engineering work — no internals, no resets-from-memory.
targetAudience: Developers who use Git but have never learned it systematically
objectives: Stage, commit, and amend changes confidently
Work with branches and remotes without fear of losing work
Use git log and git blame to investigate code history

modules.title: Day-to-Day Commits
modules.description: The commands you'll run dozens of times a day.

lessons.title: Status, Add, Commit
lessons.description: The inner loop of every Git session.
lessons.content: Run git status before every commit. Use git add -p to stage hunk-by-hunk instead of whole files. git commit -m for a one-liner, or no flag to open your editor for a longer message.
keyPoints: git status is cheap — run it constantly
git add -p gives surgical control over what lands in each commit
A commit's first line is a subject (max 72 chars), then blank, then body

lessons.title: Diff and Stash
lessons.description: See what you're about to commit — and set it aside if needed.
lessons.content: git diff shows unstaged changes; git diff --cached shows what's staged. git stash shelves your current changes so you can switch branches cleanly, and git stash pop brings them back.
keyPoints: git diff has two main views: unstaged vs staged
git stash is safe — nothing is lost, just moved aside
Name stashes with git stash push -m 'message' so you don't lose track

lessons.title: Amend and Reword
lessons.description: Fix the last commit without creating a new one.
lessons.content: git commit --amend folds new staged changes into the last commit, or lets you edit the message. Only amend commits you haven't pushed yet — amending a pushed commit rewrites history on the remote.
keyPoints: --amend rewrites the most recent commit's SHA
Never amend commits that other people may have pulled
--amend --no-edit keeps the existing message

lessons.title: Ignore and Clean
lessons.description: Keep build artefacts out of the repo.
lessons.content: A .gitignore file tells Git which paths to skip. git clean -n previews which untracked files would be removed; git clean -f actually removes them. Keep secrets in .env files and ignore them.
keyPoints: .gitignore rules are glob-based and directory-scoped
git clean -n is always safe — preview before destroying
Files already tracked must be removed with git rm --cached before .gitignore takes effect

modules.title: Branches and Remotes
modules.description: Work in parallel without stepping on anyone.
objectives: Push and pull from a shared remote

lessons.title: Branching Basics
lessons.content: git switch -c feat/my-feature creates and checks out a branch in one step. Branches in Git are essentially pointers — creating one is almost free.
keyPoints: git switch replaces the older git checkout for branch switching
Branch names should match your team's convention (feat/, fix/, chore/)
git branch -d deletes a merged branch; -D forces deletion of unmerged

lessons.title: Push and Pull
lessons.description: Share your branch with the team.
lessons.content: git push -u origin feat/my-feature pushes and sets up tracking. After that, git push and git pull operate on the tracked remote branch without extra arguments.
keyPoints: The -u flag is only needed on first push of a new branch
git pull is git fetch + git merge (or rebase, if configured)
Prefer git pull --ff-only to avoid accidental merge commits

lessons.title: Merge vs Rebase
lessons.description: Two ways to bring changes together.
lessons.content: Merge preserves the full history with a merge commit. Rebase rewrites your commits onto the target branch, producing a linear history. Teams usually pick one convention.
keyPoints: Never rebase commits that have been shared with others
git rebase -i lets you squash, reorder, or edit commits before sharing
A merge commit has two parents; a rebased commit has one — the history shape differs

lessons.title: Fetching Without Merging
lessons.description: Update your view of the remote without touching local work.
lessons.content: git fetch updates your remote-tracking branches (like origin/main) but leaves your local branches alone. You can inspect what changed before deciding to merge.
keyPoints: git fetch is always safe — it never modifies working-tree files
git log ..origin/main shows what you'd pull before pulling
Fetching daily helps you avoid stale branch surprises

quiz.question: Which command previews what git clean would remove without actually removing anything?
quiz.explanation: git clean -n (short for --dry-run) lists the untracked files that would be deleted without touching them.

modules.title: Conflicts and Investigation
modules.description: Resolve conflicts and dig into history when something breaks.
objectives: Use git log to trace changes
Use git blame to find the commit that introduced a line

lessons.title: Resolving Conflicts
lessons.description: What those <<<<<<< markers mean and how to clear them.
lessons.content: When Git can't merge two changes automatically, it writes both versions into the file with markers. Edit the file to keep what you want, delete the markers, then git add and git commit to finish the merge.
keyPoints: The markers are HEAD (your branch) vs the incoming branch name
Don't panic — conflict markers are just text, editable like any code
git merge --abort backs you out cleanly if you've dug yourself in deep

lessons.title: git log and git blame
lessons.description: Ask Git who wrote what, when, and why.
lessons.content: git log --oneline --graph shows commit history as a compact ASCII tree. git blame file.ts annotates each line with the commit that last touched it — use git show <sha> on that commit to read the message and full diff.
keyPoints: git log -S 'string' finds commits that added or removed a specific string
git blame tells you who, when, and which commit — not why
The commit message is where 'why' lives; invest time in writing good ones
